comments.title: Divination on Breaking the Egg
comments.content:
I was recently consider committing to practicing the Heathen Golden Dawn rituals you detail here. I had started doing the LBRP and was considering taking up the Middle Pillar soon, but I remembered you talking about how that's generally the point at which you "break the egg" and commit yourself to living a magical life. So did a geomancy divination:

Q: Should I "break the the egg," magically?

1st House: Puer
2nd House: Caput Draconis
3rd House: Tristitia
4th House: Aquisitio
5th House: Amissio
6th House: Puer
7th House: Rubeus
8th House: Puer
9th House: Amissio
10th House: Puer
11th House: Caput Draconis
12th House: Carcer

RW: Caput Draconis
LW: Cauda Draconis
Judge: Carcer

Looking at the 1st and 8th house, I have perfection by occupation: both Puer, suggesting the answer is "Yes!" However, the Court seems to be issuing a warning: once you do this, this is going to be for the long haul (Caput to Carcer to Cauda), so you might want to wait (Carcer).

I cast the Reconciler, which is Rubeus, which seems like it's not a good figure for the long term result. That, and the figure occupying the 1st and 8th is Puer, which suggests that I'm maybe being a bit hasty.

Additionally, Puer passes to 6th and 10th house, suggesting that my career (I'm currently trying to get back into academia, one of the workplaces most notoriously hostile to wrongthink) and my health (both physical and mental, I suspect) need to be considered or will be impacted. The interpretation there seems pretty straightforward, too: wait until I'm in a better position in my life.

I also just noticed that Rubeus in the 7th house is in company demi-simple with Puer in the 8th, which could, I think, suggest that my romantic life also needs to be considered here.

All in all, I think this might be saying that what I need right now is to focus on the more mundane aspects of life, then come back to this later. (I'm reminded of the old rule that to study Cabala, you needed to be 40 years old and married. Obviously that's not true for everyone, but maybe I should adopt it for myself.) This seems to be born out by Projection of Points, which points to the 4th house, the house of home and settling down.

Interestingly, the Part of Fortune points to the 8th house, the house of magic itself -- perhaps a different sort of magic, like natural magic, or sigil or runic magic, is more my speed right now? -- but also of death. I admit that it had already occurred to me that the indication of trouble with the 6th house of health might mean that I'll know it's time to get serious about cabalistic magic when I have reason to think I'm not too long for this world.

My questions:

1a) Is there anything I've misunderstood or missed here?

1b) In particular, is the fact that Puer in the 6th and 10th are both in company capitular with Amissio significant? That would further indicate the risk of loss in those domains, as well emphasize the aspect of academia (9th house of higher education) -- not sure what exactly the significance of the 5th house is here.

2a) Since it looks like I'm going to be waiting on the Middle Pillar, should I also hold off on the LBRP, or would that be safe? I figure the egg's already cracked a little, so some form of regular banishing ritual would be useful.

2b) Would switching from the LBRP to the SoP be advisable?

3) Am I correct in thinking that remaining a speculative mage (i.e. continuing to read and perhaps medidate on this stuff) for now would be safe?

replies.title: Re: Divination on Breaking the Egg
replies.content:
1) It looks to me as though it's saying that you're going to do it, whatever your current conscious intention may be, so you might as well get used to it. Having begun regular practice of the LBRP is generally enough.

"For mark! no sooner was I fairly found
Pledged to the plain, after a pace or two,
Than, pausing to throw backward a last view
O'er the safe road, 'twas gone; grey plain all round:
Nothing but plain to the horizon's bound.
I might go on; nought else remained to do."

2) Nope and nope.

3) Nope. You're already there having rushed ahead (Puer). That choice is no longer yours to make; all you can do is keep going.

comments.title: Pisces and the Last Days
comments.content:
I think I found another astrological pattern in religious beliefs: a Piscean belief that we are living in the last age:


  • this is the Kali Yuga (Hinduism)
  • Mahavira was the last Tirthankara (Jainism)
  • Mohammed is the last prophet (Islam)
  • End of the world coming soon (Christianity; also John the Baptist being the last prophet)
  • Joseph Smith opening last dispensation (Mormonism)
  • this is the Dharma-Ending Age (Mahayana Buddhism)
  • The Aztec belief (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Five_Suns#Fifth_sun) that this age is the last one -- made possible only by the gods sacrificing themselves, and sustained by human sacrifice -- after which the world will be destroyed.


Mind you, the Mahavira one plausibly predates the Age of Pisces, since Jainism predates it, but as with many Buddhist doctrines, it seems like it would be hard to prove when that idea got added to the religion.

I think this one is straightforward: Pisces is the last Zodiac sign in the usual order.

replies.title: 9 Virtues
replies.content: Last week, you mentioned distilling various sources down to 9 virtues. Would you mind sharing what those virtues are?

replies.title: Re: 9 Virtues
replies.content:
I don't mind at all! For idiosyncratic reasons I can go into if you're interested, all 9 virtues have a Germanic-derived name, so for some, it might sound a bit odd. I've included a brief description. If you're interested in some of the sources, I can also list those and talk about the process I went through to distill the below.

Head Group
1. Groundedness - Humility, calm, patience.
2. Truth - learn what is true about the world and share it, even if it's hard.
3. Wisdom - Apply what you know practically and productively, and recognize the value in worthwhile things you can't analyze or explain.

Chest Group
4. Love - Be kind, hospitable, and cheerful. Recognize your bonds with your fellow man and cultivate them, whether romantic, friendly, official, or anything else.
5. Troth - Keep your word and do your duty honorably.
6. Worth - Similar to the Greek concept of "arete" - excellence, value, greatness. Strive to do great things for themselves and for what they make you become, not for what you get from them. I often think of this as the "master virtue" that holds all the others when properly balanced.

Belly Group
7. Hunger - Strive to get what you want and make yourself more effective.
8. Guts - Face your fears and do what scares you.
9. Grit - Discipline, determination, stubbornness. Tough things out and see hard things through. Don't quit.

comments.content: As an exercise, I decided to memorize Tom Bombadil’s lines you quoted in your August the tenth open post. I was expecting it to be just a stage prop, but… it hefts like a steel sword instead of one of lath… Umm… Might you be persuaded to say a few words about Tolkien’s verse and his sources? Is there something more than a bit of anti-anxiety New Thought afoot here?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, obviously, but I am a big Tolkien fan. The first thing that comes to mind with Bombadil's verse is that it features a lot of alliteration, which is characteristic of Germanic poetry. Tolkien was the chair of Anglo-Saxon (what we now call "Old English") at Oxford and was intimately familiar with not only Old English poetry, but also the wider Germanic tradition (especially Norse), and his works are pervaded with themes and motifs drawn from that tradition.

Related to the above, Tolkien liked to use Germanic-derived words wherever possible. English is a Germanic language with a ridiculous number of French/Latin/Greek-derived vocabulary thanks to the Normans and the respect for the Classics of the Renaissance/Enlightenment. "Fancy" words tend to be French/Latin/Greek in origin, whereas "grounded" words tend to be Germanic - the classic example is that the animals the farmer tends are pigs and cows, but the meat the lord eats is pork and beef. Looking at the Bombadil quote, "vanish", "mountains", and "mended" are the only French-derived words in the passage.

Metrically, it looks like he might be using dactyls and spondees, which are the meter of Homeric Epic poetry. I haven't sat down and scanned it properly, but each line seems to be groups of either two or three syllables, which would fit. That would also give it a dramatic, powerful impact.

Happy to hear from our host and anyone else with more expertise on the matter!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Tolkien saturated himself in the oldest strata of Germanic language and myth. He was fluent in every Germanic language, ancient and modern, living and dead -- the guy could translate Old Norse at sight, pray impromptu in Gothic, and offer good advice to translators of his works into modern German! Oh, and he was also fluent in Welsh, Finnish, Latin, and Greek. (He taught himself Finnish because he read the Kalevala in translation and wanted to read the original...and this happened, ahem, while he was a schoolboy.)

So when he wrote a poem he did so knowing every shade of meaning of every word back to its origins in Indo-European antiquity. He was also a devout and highly conservative Catholic, so he had a rich and deep-rooted belief in the reality of the spiritual realm. That made him a bard in the full Druidical sense of the word, one of those poets and storytellers whose words call realities into being. His verses are potent stuff.

comments.title: Gods
comments.content:
I've been reading through Ovid's Metamorphoses to try to understand the gods I'm being drawn towards praying to, and I'm wrestling with a spiritual question. I'm not looking for an absolute answer so I don't have to do any thinking, I just wanted your perspective.

Why are the gods such bastards? The story of Athena rather cruelly punishing Medusa for the "crime" of being raped really did a number on me and made me call into question any respect I had for the gods. I understand that all the stories about Zeus getting it on with all the nymphs is a lurid metaphor for the fertility that rains down onto the Earth. What I can't understand is why the gods so cruelly punish mortals for their foolish and honest mistakes, or for accepting a favour from one god but not another, or just because they're jealous. Accidentally stumble upon Diana bathing? Doesn't matter that you admire her and are very sorry, you're gonna get turned into a stag and watch your own dogs disembowl you alive. Because screw you mortal.

This is all very distressing to me, because I thought the gods were...well, "good" I guess. Maybe they have to keep the balance of the universe by using nasty things like plagues and asteroid impacts, but they are trying to keep things in balance for some greater purpose, right? I thought of them as wise elders to look up to with admirstion and wonder. Most myths however, lurid penny-dreadful embellishments aside, seem to paint the gods as a bunch of immature tyrants that heap misery upon mortals for no reason whatsoever and reward loyalty with either a horrible death or a fate worse than death. Accepting a gift from a god can draw unending misery and horror from other gods, but refusing a gift can enrage the gift giver. It seems like the only rational choice is to treat them like the Sumerians treated demons of pestillence and plague - beg them to leave you alone, and pack up everything and flee when they won't.

I feel deep down that I must be mistaken somehow. Surely the divine is not petty and cruel. Surely the gods don't enjoy punishing mortals for the "crime" of being victimized by other gods.

Thus far I've resolved this conflict by believing that myths about victims being punished for being victims or children dying horrible deaths because of something their parents did are innaccurate, and that symbolic mysteries about nature have been twisted by one too many poets with political ambitions and the need to "put a new spin on things." What's your perspective?

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:
Ovid is not a source to read if you want to understand why people worshipped the Greek and Roman gods. He was a cynical old materialist who didn't believe in the gods, and for all the literary beauty of his work, in religious terms it's a bit like what you'd get if Sam Harris or some other famous atheist rewrote the Bible.

To the Greeks, the gods were not good in the sense of being moral, they were good in the sense of being excellent. Each one embodies one of the creative forces of the cosmos in its pure and intense form, which means they're very difficult to handle in the human world! The later Greek pagans argued that you should never take the myths literally -- "myths are things that never happened but always are," wrote Sallust, and he insisted that their very absurdity was made to wake up the mind and get you thinking about their real meanings.

(My mind suggests "completeness" as a candidate for the equivalent Aquarian virtue, though perhaps the archaic spelling "compleatness" would be more fitting.)

- pretentious_username

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content: It's entirely a function of astrological ages. Each age has its own way of experiencing divinity, which is why at the change of ages one pantheon so often gives way to another. That's why the transition from one age to another is such a jumble, religiously speaking.

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:
As far as good and evil acts or even gods, could you say sins vary from each individual person? Their sin is going against what their "gut" is telling them. Not doing what they are supposed to in this incarnation. I mean that too in a sense that has nothing to do with what society or even a small group of people may consider wrong.

As an extreme example, I think most people agree, you shouldn't murder someone, but what if that was someones path in some incarnation that will help learn a lesson in the next? Even on an animal level, the predator may kill its prey, which was it's path, but the prey's sin was being killed and not outsmarting the predator. Flip it around. The predators sin was being outsmarted and starving, while the prey followed it's path by escaping.

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:
Sin in the modern sense wasn't even a concept in classical religion. There were actions that angered the gods, and it was a bad idea to do them, but as often as not they had to do with impurity rather than ethics.

The approach you've suggested is the one found in the Bhagavad Gita -- sin is a failure to rise up to your dharma, your duty and purpose in life. It was Arjuna's dharma to go out there onto the battlefield and kill people, even though the other side included many of his own relatives and friends, and so refusing to fight -- a virtue for other people in other situations -- would have been a sin for him.

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content: interesting, I am not familiar with the Bhagavad Gita, other than my brother is studying it.  the concept came to my while meditating on the Resh path, which I found a little out of place, but put a side note for future ponderings.

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:
Not specific to the Greco-Roman Gods, and perhaps not as helpful as JMG's answer, but one perspective I've found enlightening for understanding myth is one that Jordan Peterson explains with regard to the Old Testament in his lectures on Genesis: the God of the Old Testament isn't so much a benevolent person as he is the embodiment of the universe as experienced by a bunch of hard-working ancient farmers/herders. Sure, this is colored by a monotheist approach to understanding, which has some issues for a polytheist, and there was still the insistence that things were "good" overall, so it doesn't totally address your concern.

What I have found helpful is the idea that the Gods are Gods of this world, and the world is the way it is - including all the crappy parts. Trying to understand "good" as only that which we as mortals with limited understanding would choose is potentially missing nuance or hidden meaning.

For example, victims being punished for what happened to them might not so much be a recommendation for policy as it is a recognition of what tends to happen in the world - something horrible to learn from rather than a playbook to copy.

Anyhow, I hope this is helpful, and I hope you find a path to a relationship with some Gods you can feel good about, whatever that might look like.

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content:
I’ve come to regard the myths as the Kabuki theater of the gods. They don’t embody wisdom; they mirror our flaws, and direct our gaze towards wisdom. The Hellenistic gods and goddesses strike me as fundamentally beneficent, and as deities that are truly real and present.

And I don’t trust Ovid much for the reasons JMG gives. Similarly, Homer had his own agenda (a most noble one I might add), and so he didn’t hesitate to drag the gods and goddess through the mud. Plato, devout as he was, despised him for that.

But yeah, I’d long had the objections you have, but I found the myths too magnetic to ignore, and for all the distortion, the beneficence of those deities shines through for me. (And JMG's reply above feels fully compatible.)

Then one day, Hermes tapped me on the shoulder…

—Lunar Apprentice

comments.title: Child Roland and the Dark Tower
comments.content:
Above you quoted to me a verse from Robert Browning's "Childe Roland to the Dark Tower Came." I had to look that up, since I'd either never read the poem before, or read it for a literature class assignment that I was paying no attention to.

The version I found and read: https://www.sparknotes.com/poetry/browning/section8/

Ignoring the pretentious modernist hogwash that is the commentary...

It clicked as I read stanzas XI and XII that the gray plain was not some alien landscape into which Roland had been tricked: it's the real world after you see through the illusion of conventionality. The safe road never existed.

I'm assuming that Browning was no stranger to occultism -- many artists of that era seem to have been up to their eyeballs in it. I feel like the poem has more secrets to unlock. (In particular, I'd have expected one more inversion of the landscape, following the blowing of the horn: from wasteland back to lush green, representing both Rolande's mastery of himself and his newfound wisdom: "to arrive where we started and to know the place for the first time." But then, the horn is not the grail...)

replies.title: Re: Child Roland and the Dark Tower
replies.content: The poem has a great deal to unlock, but it's not a conventional poem. Browning liked to write poems that express a particular emotional state with extraordinary richness and precision, and he was also writing with an eye toward other poetry -- behind Childe Roland lies the whole panoply of Victorian Arthuriana, full of fake emotion and fake glory. So he showed the state of mind, moment by moment, of the weary, battered, driven knight who finally accomplishes his quest. That last sentence in quotes is what the romance tells; the rest is what it was actually like at the time...

replies.title: Re: Child Roland and the Dark Tower
replies.content: I read it - just the first once-over - as a commentary on the aftermath of war.

replies.title: Re: Child Roland and the Dark Tower
replies.content:
In my English honours class in first year university, we read Porphyria's Lover and were asked to interpret it in class. It was as mortifying an exercise of pretentious undergraduate Deepness as one can imagine. I said it sounded like a Victoria poet who was making a rather bitter point about the era's faith. It was the last line. The TA told me I was wrong, and it had nothing to do with religion, nothing! I can't even remember what he said the correct answer was.

(I did not, by any means, respond maturely by immediately going out and buying the collected works of Browning, with an introduction in it that said that shifting religious beliefs was a core topic of Browning's work. comments.title: Human Incarnation
comments.content:
I imagine it could vary significantly, but what are your thoughts in regard to how long the average person spends in human incarnations? I was learning about Doggerland the other day, the submerged area of land now under the North Sea. I admit I'm still a bit of a skeptic about the whole concept of reincarnation, but I was thinking to myself, "What if I lived there? What if I spent a whole life in the fields and forests of a land now under the sea?" But it was flooded over 8,000 years ago (give or take). Would it be more likely that a human in 2021 may have been a squirrel, badger, or mosquito in 8,000 BC?

Christopher K.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: It really depends on what you've been doing with your human lives.  As far as I can tell I wasn't human that far back, but if you messed up badly enough several times, or ended up taking a chunk of time out of incarnation, it's possible.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: What were you?

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: Lots of things, like everyone else.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content:
a) What sort of things could lead to you messing up badly enough?

b) Why would souls spend a long time out of incarnation?

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content:
1) It's a long, long list. You can get into some kind of karmic tangle with other people that takes many lifetimes to resolve, for example, or you can screw up badly enough that it's going to take you many lives to work out the karma.

3) Again, there are lots of ways. For example, you could get stuck in the first stage of the afterlife and stay a ghost for a long time, or you could do certain practices to try to prolong your existence out of the body, or it could just be a very long time before a body suited to your needs comes open.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: One could look at it as a screw up, but depending on one views free will it could also be a strategic repositioning that allows the "delinquent" to help as many people as possible come through a very difficult bottleneck. Those who are helped are no less appreciative, and in some cases might thank their lucky stars that a certain strand of evolution was delayed for their benefit.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: Excluding such outliers, about what period do you think the older of the current crop of human souls got started? Or put another way, is there an earliest particular period that you hear frequently mentioned by credible people who remember a number of their past lives?

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: That's remarkably hard to determine. Remember that nobody in 5000 BC had wall calendars on the walls of their huts saying that it was 5000 BC!  The earliest life I remember was more or less neolithic -- I was a member of a tribal people in a jungle setting with tigers, so it was south or southeast Asia, but that could have been any time between 20,000 BC and the first more or less datable life I can recall, which was in the European Dark Ages. My memories don't help at all -- as I recall, the way people denoted years was "the year the rains came late and my mother's maternal cousin Mau'hi had twins," and so on.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: How was life in the Dark Ages?  In the last ten years or so, a lot of historians seem to be re-evaluating them as not so dark after all.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: It seemed perfectly normal at the time. Food wasn't always abundant, violence was pretty pervasive, and a nice place to sleep on the floor not too far from the fire with a belly full of pease porridge and bread was the height of luxury, but since that's what I grew up with in that life and everyone else I knew but the local warlord, his family, and a few of his vassals lived the same way, it never occurred to me to grumble.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation
replies.content: I had a life in Roman Syria, but I don't remember it. There are reasons for that. Where it fits in relation to the life in the jungle I couldn't tell you.

replies.title: Re: Human Incarnation

comments.title: Rain
comments.content: A few days ago, I was caught in a severe storm with heavy lightning that broke a 2-1/2 week drought. It was hot (95°!) prior to the rain, and there was lightning nearly overhead at times. Far from cleansing, my arms and eyes seemed to burn from the rain. I rushed home and took a cold shower and the sensation receded. I recall you saying cold water was ethericly cleansing. Is there something different going on with rain from a storm?

replies.title: Re: Rain
replies.content:
Hi there,

Lightening can break up regular oxygen molecules to create ozone. Regular oxygen molecules are made of happy pairs of oxygen atoms. Ozone molecules are unhappy triplets with an unpaired electron that tends to rip and tear at anything it can get at.

I suspect the heavy lightening created lots of ozone that the rain then brought to earth. It can be very irritating to eyes and lungs; that's probably why it burned you.

-Ms. Krieger

comments.title: Imagination and Feeling
comments.content:
Hello,

I ask this admitting it might not make sense, but I have questions darn it! I'm starting to understand why a lot of magical literature I've read so far states it's best to learn under a mentor.

I've been performing the LBRP and MPR daily for about four months. Initially I focused on the procedure, vocalizations and visualizations (naturally). When it came to visualizing, I was attacking it with a "fake it til you make it" approach. Though not perfect at all, there are improvements in the visualizations.

However over the last couple weeks, I've noticed that I'm starting to pick up on "feeling" some of the energy. For example, instead of just moving the shaft of light downward in the MPR, I'm starting to sense it as well. The thing is, the movement is a little wonky. It's almost as if I can feel how out of tune my etheric body is. When I focus on the crown, I can sense it whirling a bit lopsided. When I move an arc of light, I can sense it shooting off in different directions. When I focus on the center beam of light, I can feel it shifting. And for some reason there is some sort of blockage to feeling and visualization on the left side of my field.

It is starting to throw off my visualizations a bit. My question is - at this time, would it be better for me to strictly try to keep my mind on the visualizations and not follow these feelings? Or would it be a better idea to incorporate the feelings more with the visualizations and try to get them to move in tandem?

Thank you for your consideration!

replies.title: Re: Imagination and Feeling
replies.content: For now, concentrate on the visualizations and don't let yourself be distracted by the clairvoyant perceptions you're getting. Later on, you can explore the energy flows.

replies.title: Re: Imagination and Feeling
replies.content: (Not the OP) Hmm, interesting. I have been doing the (LBRC+)MP for about a year and a half now. I had taken it as given that imagining the feelings was part of the "visualization" aspect of this, and have encountered something similar to what the OP describes: the feelings don't quite follow where I want them, being off consistently in certain ways. A change in one of those (actually in how the visual part behaved) seemed to predict getting sick a couple of days later. I have been assuming, though, that the idea was to try to get everything in sync and following the descriptions consistently, symmetrically, in the right places etc. Should I instead be focusing more on the visuals? What point is "later on" as far as exploring energy flows, and is there anything in particular to look for, or any particularly recommended book to look at?

replies.title: Re: Imagination and Feeling
replies.content: Most people seem to find it best to work at the visualizations until they're strong and stable, and then pay more attention to the felt energy flows. Your mileage may vary, of course.

replies.content:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Here's a few recommendations for critical thinking skills:

1. "Mental Models" are a concept that was popularized by Charlie Munger (Warren Buffet's business partner) in his various talks and collected in his book "Poor Charlie's Almanack". The basic concept is that you identify helpful patterns of thinking from multiple fields of study/thinking, and then train yourself to apply those patterns elsewhere. The presentation I'm most familiar with is that of Farnam Street (https://fs.blog/mental-models/). The online articles are a great start, but the same blog has also put out a series of lovely hardback books called "The Great Mental Models" that are newly-written on the same concepts, and I find the explanations in the book a bit clearer and more helpful. There are also a lot of other sources on "mental models" you might find by searching around.

2. Closely related, "Seeking Wisdom: from Darwin to Munger" focuses on patterns of thinking, especially those aimed at reducing common ways of being wrong. I have had a copy for a few years, but haven't read it yet.

3. "Systemantics - The Systems Bible" is an enjoyably sarcastic guide to systems thinking, which is an especially helpful flavor of critical thinking.

4. Approach this one with caution, as it is just about ground zero for aggressive scientific materialism, but "The Sequences (Rationality A-Z)" at Less Wrong (https://www.lesswrong.com/rationality) include a lot of careful thinking about thinking. I got pretty into these before a personal religious experience turned me off to skeptical materialism, but I think they are still an extensive resource on how to think carefully (if you can put up with a fair amount of aggressive metaphysical assumptions).

Would you say it’s possible to establish the required faith and/or belief in magic if one isn’t born with such certainty? If so, how might one do this in an intellectually honest way?

In other words, are you saying approaching magical systems with a tentative, experimental mindset would likely sabotage the results?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've found that every single time without exception that somebody comes to me saying "I want to prove to myself that magic works before I commit to it," they're not going to commit no matter what happens. Are you familiar at all with the psychological theory of interpersonal games? This game is called "Prove It To Me," and the player's strategy is always to insist that whatever proof you offer isn't adequate. That bores me, so I don't play along.

If you want to see if magic works, half an hour of practice a day for the next year, following a reputable system of training will do it. If you aren't prepared to do that, you'd be wasting your time studying magic anyway.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As a point of comparison, when I first tried magical practice, I was theoretically open to it being "real", but I was pretty convinced it was just a way to interact with my own subconscious. I didn't get much in way of results except getting okay at divination with runes.

A few months ago, I read our host's World Full of Gods and decided to make a good faith effort at acting "as if" Gods and magic were real and to commit to a daily practice. Within a few weeks, I had a rather strong religious experience while meditating, came to Magic Monday in a panic and asked about what had happened and got a "exactly what it seemed like, dummy" response (not that harsh, of course). Then and since, I've found old habits of thought creeping in to try to explain it as "only in my head" or "it could have been X instead" or the like, but the experience itself was so self-evidently beneficial that I've been able to keep up the good faith effort, and I've been noticing more and more weird stuff.

Anyhow, that's all to say that if you can at least get to a point where you really are open to the possibility that it could work and you start doing the work, you might get some "confirmation" along the way.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may add to what JMG has said, the more someone is nervous that they're too X, the more likely they're actually repressing their capacity to be X, or have otherwise poorly integrated it, and so it's coming out at odd angles. (Look up scrupulosity sometime and try to imagine an actually-bad person suffering from it!)

That isn't to say that you're not open-minded enough: what I think is going on is that you're not yet really comfortable yet with your capacity to form your own beliefs that are at odds with the materialist worldview. In which case, the solution is definitely not to try to prove anything to yourself or to even to take a leap of faith. It's too get comfortable with thinking for yourself.

What has worked for me and a number of other people I'm aware of is allowing yourself to really be aware of the places where the materialist worldview seems to make no sense. To take a proverb from one of the more thoughtful subset of materialists, notice your confusion — don't just accept that since everyone else acts like there's no problem, that there isn't one.

Look for places where the materialist explanation is a blatant dodge or thought stopper. One common trick is what's been called "promissory materialism," where the materialist stance on an issue boils down to "No, we can't explain it yet, but that doesn't mean it's magic!" (Well, no, it doesn't mean it's magic. Now let's get back to how this makes no sense if materialism is true and a great deal of sense if it's false…)

replies.title: Re: Greek SoP
replies.content:
East: Apollo
West: Dionysos
South: Athena
North: Aphrodite
Above: Zeus
Below: Demeter

Before you do this, fill a glass with good red wine and go outside. Say the names of all six deities and then say, "I ask for your blessings in my work with the Sphere of Protection. May this offering be acceptable to you!" Then pour all the wine on the ground.

This is called "pouring a libation" and it's a classic way to invoke the Greek gods and ask for their help. If you want to continue to build a relationship with them, pour libations to them on the solstices and the equinoxes and thank them for the blessings and help they have given you.

Here are two basic guides:

https://merrilynhope.com/how-to-make-home-made-homeopathic-remedies/
http://oaks.nvg.org/home-remedies.html

It's really simple. You can get the chemicals themselves very cheaply from a variety of sources. Once you have them, mix 1 part chemical with 9 parts inert substance (alcohol and lactose are common, but you can use anything edible) and shake thoroughly for ten minutes or so, either by hand or using some kind of mechanical device. Start with a tiny bit of the original substance and you've got pounds and pounds of cell salt; the best approach is to make up a 3x batch and then do 6x batches from that as needed.

comments.title: Materialist Herestetics
comments.content:
In writing my reply to the skeptical anon above, something clicked: materialism depends crucially on the use of heresthetics:

https://www.dictionary.com/browse/heresthetic

"a political strategy by which a person or group sets or manipulates the context and structure of a decision-making process in order to win or be more likely to win"

The politics in question are, of course, the cultural politics of the elite, but the definition fits: starting with Hume, then with academia in the 1800's, and more recently in the New Atheist era, materialists went to great lengths to cast themselves as the "default position" or "null hypothesis" — the worldview that wins by default if the other side cannot meet the burden of proof. Once you grant that, they need only keep moving the field goals.

It's the same sort of tactics that kept neoliberalism cemeented firmly in place for so long — "There is no alternative," anyone?

Seen in this light, the New Atheism was a smaller version of the #Resistance (and let's face it, they pioneered many of the tactics that online activists now use). For them, 9/11, the Religious Right, the Intelligent Design movement, and to a lesser extent the bump in the popularity of astrology, Neopaganism and other forms of "woo" had a similar impact as Trump and the deplorables*: it showed that the old heresthetics had failed, and religion was not going to quietly go away like it promised.

* Which of course isn't to say they're comparable in every way. 9/11 was a genuine tragedy, the Religious Right had some really scary authoritarian tendencies — including a rather self-serving understanding of the Constitution, though to their credit they at least paid lip service to it — and teaching ID was always a Constitutionally-iffy affair. Also pop-astrology and pop-Wicca are basically just tacky ways to get laid. None of which is the point of what I'm saying.

replies.title: Re: Materialist Herestetics
replies.content: I wasn't familiar with the word, but the rhetorical strategy -- yeah, exactly.

So it would go Odin > Frey > Freya > Thor > Tyr > Baldur > Frigga > Bragi > Heimdall/Skadi > Idun . What do you think? comments.title: Odr
comments.content:
Looking at the Wiki page on Odr — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%93%C3%B0r — something hit me: his name literally refers to the divine fury that Odin is the master of, suggesting he's perhaps a personification of the primal divine force.

Freya is his wife, suggesting Freya has an intimate connection to that primal divine force — which makes sense, as she's the goddess of magic and the seith trance, and is something of a divine ideal of the magician and seithwoman.

The Prose Edda, but not the Poetic Edda, has Odr leaving her, and her crying tears of crimson and gold over the loss, and wandering the world to find him again. The Prose Edda was written well into the Age of Pisces and well after the silencing of the oracles, so perhaps this reflects that latter development.

In other words, perhaps Freya's loss and search represent the distancing of spiritual forces from humanity and the need ever since for occultists to scour the world for lore that will reconnect them to the Divine. This also gives a different meaning to Freya's promiscuity: occultists and magicians are to take wisdom where we can find it, not by committing to One True Way.

replies.title: Re: Odr
replies.content: Hmm! I like this. A good colorful use of mythological language.

comments.title: Fiction, Tulpas, Archetypes and Egregores
comments.content:
The questions, before I run off on a tangent: In crafting fiction works, is there a spiritual concern to getting overinvested in a specific character? Does the answer or nature of the concern change if the character is based in mythology or have a significant literary legacy?

Background: I was invited a few years ago, around 2018, to participate in a tabletop RPG campaign set in medieval fantasy - meets-greek mythology-meets hogwarts. We were encouraged as part of the setting to choose deities whose causes we would champion. I was the only pagan in the group, and reluctant as I was to involve worship and reverence of the deity I felt closest to in a game, I chose a deity for my character with whom I kept a more... irreverent association.

This began a sort of metagame between myself and the Christian game master, where he would dive into the weeds of the Greek mythos to keep me guessing at what was going on and my bard would order greek tales in such a way to resemble familiar Christian tales. Down the research rabbit hole I fell and at the bottom came across... something. An obscure personification, whose perspective I find fascinating, and who hasn't left my thoughts for a day even these years later. Especially these times.

I wonder now if I was careless in my initial choosing. LBRP mitigates some more destructive influences associated with the traditional telling, but I have difficulty now writing characters other than this one, or stories other than this story. I can keep my own mind and question my own thoughts, but it's like I often view thing automatically through two different color lenses. Besides the writers block there aren't any handicaps that I've noticed, so maybe this isn't a big deal, and I'm making something out of nothing. I've been trying to read CG Jung lately but keep getting mired in current events, another symptom of Delusion or of the times? I wonder if there is a distinction anymore...

Many thank yous to our Host and the broader Ecosophy community for your consideration and insight.

Addie Ilios

replies.title: Re: Fiction, Tulpas, Archetypes and Egregores
replies.content: The deity probably won't leave you alone until you write the story. That happens!

On a practical level, may I offer some advice? Don't try to get an agent or to place it with a big publisher. That's the easiest way to fail these days. There are dozens of small to mid-sized publishers that don't require you to go through an agent, and they're much more open to interesting new options. Go to a good children's bookstore, look for books that are more or less similar to yours, note down the publishers -- get a good collection of them -- and then go visit their websites. If their submission requirements say you don't need an agent, consider them.
